title: Max Independent Set (5-Node Graph)
summary: Pick the largest set of nodes from a graph such that no two chosen nodes share an edge. 5 qubits.
problem_statement: I have a graph with 5 nodes labeled 0 through 4 and edges (0,1), (0,2), (1,3), (2,3), (3,4). Pick a subset of nodes such that no two chosen nodes are connected by an edge, and the subset is as large as possible. Tell me which nodes are chosen.
real_world_example: Choosing conference talks to attend when many overlap in time — each talk is a node, edges connect overlapping talks, and you want to see as many as possible. Or selecting compatible medications from a list where some pairs interact.

expected_pattern.variables: x_i in {0, 1} — whether node i is chosen
expected_pattern.objective: maximize sum(x_i) - λ * sum over edges (i,j) of x_i * x_j (penalty for choosing both endpoints)
expected_pattern.constraints: encoded into the objective via the pairwise penalty term; no explicit constraints

expected_solution_summary: Optimal choice: {1, 2, 4} — three nodes with no edge between any pair (1–2, 1–4, 2–4 are all non-edges). Verify by inspection: no edge in the graph has both endpoints in this set.
learning_notes: This is a classic 'penalty formulation' problem. There's no hard constraint — instead, we penalize any pair of chosen adjacent nodes heavily enough that the maximizer will never pick both. Choosing the penalty weight λ correctly is the interesting design decision; too small and the solver picks invalid sets, too large and the objective landscape becomes flat.
